<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During 2002, the Company recorded
$93.4&nbsp;million of goodwill related to the acquisition of
Vality Technology, Inc. (&#147;Vality&#148;). During 2002, the
Company also recorded a $0.9&nbsp;million related to the
adjustment of deferred taxes and $0.2&nbsp;million related to
the adjustment of deferred compensation recorded through the
Company&#146;s acquisition of Torrent in November 2001 (see
Note&nbsp;12 to theses Notes to Consolidated Financial
Statements).
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">Other intangible assets amounted to
$11.1&nbsp;million (net of accumulated amortization of
$6.9&nbsp;million) and $7.2&nbsp;million (net of accumulated
amortization of $2.2&nbsp;million) at December&nbsp;31, 2002 and
December&nbsp;31, 2001, respectively. These intangible assets
consist primarily of customer lists and patented technology
acquired through business combinations, and the Company is
amortizing these assets over their estimated useful lives,
generally three years. There are no expected residual values
related to these intangible assets. Amortization expense for the
years ended December&nbsp;31, 2002, 2001 and 2000 was
$4.6&nbsp;million, $1.8&nbsp;million and $5.1&nbsp;million,
respectively. Estimated future amortization expense, assuming no
future impairment charges or acquisitions, would be as follows
(in thousands):
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><FONT size="2">Note 12&nbsp;&#151; Business
Combinations</FONT></B>

<DIV>&nbsp;</DIV>

<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0">

<TR>
    <TD width="3%"></TD>
    <TD width="97%"></TD>
</TR>

<TR valign="top">
    <TD></TD>
    <TD>
    <B><I><FONT size="2">Pooling-of-interests
    combination</FONT></I></B></TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On March&nbsp;1, 2000, the Company completed its
acquisition of Ardent, a provider of data integration
infrastructure software for data warehouse, business
intelligence, and e-business applications. In the acquisition,
the former shareholders of Ardent received 3.5 shares of Common
Stock in exchange for each outstanding share of Ardent common
stock held by such shareholder (the &#147;Ardent Merger&#148;).
An aggregate of 70,437,000 shares of Common Stock were issued
pursuant to the Ardent Merger, and an aggregate of 17,174,000
options to purchase Ardent common stock were assumed by the
Company. The Ardent Merger was accounted for as a
pooling-of-interests combination and, accordingly, the
consolidated financial statements for periods prior to the
combination have been restated to include the accounts and
results of operations of Ardent and all intercompany
transactions have been eliminated.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">No adjustments were necessary to conform to
accounting policies of the combined entities.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;<I>Purchase
combinations</I></FONT></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On April&nbsp;3, 2002, the Company acquired
Vality, a private company that specialized in enterprise data
quality management. The results of Vality&#146;s operations have
been included in the consolidated financial statements since the
date of the acquisition. The Company expects that this
acquisition will enable the Company to create a market leading
offering that integrates ETL (extraction, transformation, and
load), metadata management, and data quality and data cleansing
technologies. As of December&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company has paid
the net amount of $94.2&nbsp;million to acquire Vality, which
consists of $91.4&nbsp;million paid to
</FONT>

preferred shares of Vality and vested stock options, and

(in millions):

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The portion of the purchase price allocated to
in-process research and development costs (&#147;IPRD&#148;) in
the Vality acquisition was $1.2&nbsp;million, or approximately
1% of the total purchase price. The value allocated to the
project identified as in process was charged to operations in
the second quarter of 2002.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The remaining identified intangible assets
acquired, including, without limitation, technology and
covenants not to compete between the Company and certain former
members of senior management of Vality, were assigned fair
values based upon an independent appraisal and amounted to
$8.5&nbsp;million in the aggregate. The Company believes that
these identified intangible assets have no residual value. The
excess of the purchase price over the identified tangible and
intangible assets was recorded as goodwill and amounted to
approximately $93.4&nbsp;million. The Company is in the process
of completing the purchase price allocation which may be subject
to refinement. As part of this refinement, during the three
months ended December&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company recorded an
increase to accrued merger costs to reserve for future lease
obligations related to a lease for a facility the Company exited
as part of the acquisition, and has not been able to sublet. The
existing technology is being amortized over three years and the
covenant not to compete is being amortized over one year. In
accordance with current accounting standards, the goodwill is
not being amortized and will be tested for impairment as
required by SFAS No.&nbsp;142 (See Note&nbsp;11 of these Notes
to Consolidated Financial Statements). The Company anticipates
that none of the $101.9&nbsp;million of the intangible assets
recorded in connection with the Vality acquisition will be
deductible for income tax purposes.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On November&nbsp;28, 2001, the Company acquired
Torrent, a provider of highly scalable parallel processing
infrastructure software for the development and execution of
data warehousing, business intelligence and analytical
applications. Under terms of the acquisition, the Company paid
$44.1&nbsp;million, of which $6.3&nbsp;million was paid in 2002,
to acquire all outstanding common and preferred shares of
Torrent. In addition, the Company issued options to purchase
216,000 shares of Common Stock in exchange for outstanding
options to

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The portion of the purchase price allocated to
in-process research and development costs in the Torrent
acquisition was $5.5&nbsp;million, or approximately 11% of the
total purchase price. The value allocated to the project
identified as IPRD was charged to operations in the fourth
quarter of 2001.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The remaining identified intangible assets
acquired were assigned fair values based upon an independent
appraisal and amounted to $5.2&nbsp;million. The excess of the
purchase price over the identified tangible and intangible
assets was recorded as goodwill and amounted to approximately
$38.5&nbsp;million. Total intangible assets acquired of
approximately $44.3&nbsp;million are included in
&#147;Intangible assets&#148; in the accompanying consolidated
balance sheets. The existing technology and customer list are
being amortized over three years. In accordance with current
accounting standards, the goodwill is not being amortized and is
tested for impairment on a periodic basis.
</FONT>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>

<P align="left">
<B><FONT size="2">Note 13&nbsp;&#151; Litigation</FONT></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On May&nbsp;26, 1999, the Company entered into a
memorandum of understanding regarding the settlement of pending
private securities and related litigation against the Company,
including a federal class action, a derivative action, and a
state class action. In November 1999, the settlement was
approved by the applicable federal and state courts. The
settlement resolved all material litigation arising out of the
restatement of the Company&#146;s financial statements that was
publicly announced in November 1997. In accordance with the
terms of the memorandum of understanding, the Company paid
approximately $3.2&nbsp;million in cash during the second
quarter of 1999, and an additional amount of approximately
$13.8&nbsp;million of insurance proceeds was contributed
directly by certain of the Company&#146;s insurance carriers on
behalf of certain of the Company&#146;s current and former
officers and directors. As part of the settlement, the Company
also agreed to contribute a minimum of 9.0&nbsp;million shares
of Common Stock, which was required to provide a guaranteed
value of $91.0&nbsp;million for a maximum term of one year from
the date of the final approval of the settlement by the courts.
The first distributions of shares of Common Stock occurred in
November and December of 1999 when the Company issued
approximately 2.9&nbsp;million shares to the plaintiff&#146;s
counsel. The stock price guarantee was satisfied with respect to
the first distributions of settlement shares. In April 2001, the
Company issued the remaining 6.1&nbsp;million of the minimum
9.0&nbsp;million shares to be issued under the settlement.
Pursuant to the terms of the settlement, the Company paid an
additional amount of $26.2&nbsp;million in cash in November 2001
to satisfy the stock price guarantee with respect to the
remaining 6.1&nbsp;million shares issued under the settlement.
The Company&#146;s former independent auditors, Ernst &#38;
Young LLP, paid $34.0&nbsp;million in cash. The total amount of
the settlement was $142.0&nbsp;million.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">In July 1997, the Securities and Exchange
Commission (the &#147;SEC&#148;) issued a formal order of
private investigation of the Company and certain unidentified
other entities and persons with respect to non-specified
accounting matters, public disclosures and trading activity in
the Company&#146;s securities. During the course of the
investigation, the Company learned that the investigation
concerned the events leading to the restatement of the
Company&#146;s financial statements, including fiscal years
1994, 1995 and 1996, that was publicly announced in November
1997. The Company has entered into a settlement with the SEC
regarding the investigation. Pursuant to the settlement, the
Company consented to the entry by the SEC of an Order
Instituting Public Administrative Proceedings Pursuant to
Section&nbsp;8A of the Securities Act of 1933 and
Section&nbsp;21C of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Making
Findings, and Imposing a Cease and Desist Order (the
&#147;Order&#148;). The Order was issued by the SEC on
January&nbsp;11, 2000. Pursuant to the Order, the Company
neither admitted nor denied the findings, except as to
jurisdiction, contained in the Order. The Order directs the
Company to cease and desist from committing or causing any
violation, and any future violation, of Section&nbsp;17(a) of
the Securities Act of 1933 (&#147;Securities Act&#148;),
Sections&nbsp;10(b), 13(a) and 13(b) of the Exchange Act and
Rules&nbsp;10b-5, 12b-20, 13a-1, 13a-13 and 13b2-1 under the
Exchange Act. Pursuant to the Order, the Company is also
required to cooperate in the SEC&#146;s continuing investigation
of other entities and persons.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">EXPO 2000 filed an action against Ascential
Software GmbH (the Company&#146;s German subsidiary) in the
Hanover, Germany district court in September 1998 seeking
recovery of approximately $6.0&nbsp;million, plus interest, for
breach of a sponsorship contract signed in 1997. The Company
filed a counterclaim for breach of contract, seeking recovery of
approximately $3.1&nbsp;million. In August 1999, the court
entered a judgment against the Company in the amount of
approximately $6.0&nbsp;million, although approximately
$2.1&nbsp;million of the judgment was conditioned upon the
return of certain software by EXPO 2000. The Company filed an
appeal and reserved approximately $3.1&nbsp;million for the
expected outcome of the appeal. In April 2001, the German
appellate court set aside the lower court&#146;s judgment and
issued a judgment in favor of the Company in the amount of
approximately $2.5&nbsp;million. On June&nbsp;5, 2001 EXPO filed
an appeal to the German Federal High Court in Civil Matters. The
German Federal High Court decided on May&nbsp;14, 2002 that the
appeal of EXPO would not be accepted. Therefore, the judgment of
the Higher Regional Court is final and EXPO was ordered to pay
Ascential Software GmbH the sum of approximately
$3.0&nbsp;million including 5% interest from
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-38
</FONT>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#toc">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>
</DIV>

<P align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED
FINANCIAL&nbsp;STATEMENTS&nbsp;&#151;&nbsp;(Continued)</FONT></B>

<P align="left">
<FONT size="2">January&nbsp;19, 1999. This amount was paid to
Ascential Software GmbH during the second quarter of fiscal 2002
and is recorded, net of $0.3&nbsp;million of associated tax, in
&#147;Other income, net&#148; for the year ended
December&nbsp;31, 2002. In addition, the Company reversed its
prior $3.1&nbsp;million accrual that was recorded in August 1999
and recorded a $2.8&nbsp;million decrease, adjusted for foreign
currency fluctuations, to &#147;General and administrative&#148;
expense for the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The Company is a defendant in one action filed
against Unidata, Inc., a company that the Company merged with in
1998 (&#147;Unidata&#148;), in May 1996 in the U.S. District
Court for the Western District of Washington, and was previously
a defendant in another action filed in September 1996 in the
U.S. District Court for the District of Colorado. The plaintiff,
a company controlled by a former stockholder of Unidata and a
distributor of its products in certain parts of Asia, alleged in
both actions the improper distribution of certain Unidata
products in the plaintiff&#146;s exclusive territory and
asserted damages of approximately $30.0&nbsp;million (among
other relief) under claims for fraud, breach of contract, unfair
competition, racketeering and corruption, and trademark and
copyright infringement. Unidata denied the allegations against
it in its answers to the complaints. In the Colorado action,
Unidata moved that the matter be resolved by arbitration in
accordance with its distribution agreement with the plaintiff.
In May 1999, the U.S. District Court for the District of
Colorado issued an order compelling arbitration and in September
2000, the arbitrator issued an award against the Company for
$3.5&nbsp;million plus attorneys&#146; fees and expenses
estimated to be approximately $0.8&nbsp;million.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The Company was also joined as a party in an
action in China filed against Unidata, its former distributor
and a customer, by the same plaintiff who filed the U.S. actions
against Unidata and a related company. This action, filed in the
Guangdong Provincial People&#146;s Superior Court in China,
arose out of the same facts at issue in the U.S. actions. The
Company entered into a settlement agreement with the plaintiff
pursuant to which, among other things, as each of the actions
was dismissed, the underlying license terminated, certain
intellectual property rights were assigned to the Company by the
plaintiff, and the Company was obligated to pay into escrow, and
then have released from escrow to the plaintiff, certain amounts
totaling approximately $16.0&nbsp;million to settle all claims
in all pending litigation, with the exception of potential
claims for indemnity between the Company and other co-defendants
relating to attorneys&#146; fees and related amounts, if any,
paid in settlement. Subsequently, the China and Denver actions
were dismissed; the plaintiff&#146;s claims in the Washington
State action were dismissed; the license agreement was
terminated; and the intellectual property rights were assigned
to the Company as of the end of the second quarter of fiscal
2002 and the full amount escrowed was released to plaintiff.
During the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company paid
into escrow the entire amount of the settlement of approximately
$16.0&nbsp;million.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">At the end of October 2002, the Company
instituted an arbitration proceeding with the American
Arbitration Association in Kansas City against Sprint/ United
Management Company (&#147;Sprint&#148;) for non-payment of
$6.5&nbsp;million (the &#147;Sprint Indebtedness&#148;) due and
owing under an agreement between Informix Software and Sprint
dated effective March&nbsp;31, 2000 (the &#147;Sprint
Agreement&#148;). IBM had been providing services covered by the
Sprint Agreement to Sprint pursuant to an agreement between IBM
and the Company (the &#147;operating agreement&#148;) signed at
the time of the IBM Transaction. The Company instituted the
arbitration proceeding because Sprint declined to pay any part
of the Sprint Indebtedness. The Company and Sprint executed a
settlement agreement, dated as of December&nbsp;24, 2002,
resulting in the termination of the Sprint Agreement, dismissal
of all claims, and payment by Sprint to the Company of
$4.0&nbsp;million. Pursuant to the terms of the operating
agreement, the Company in turn remitted that $4.0&nbsp;million
payment to IBM on December&nbsp;30, 2002.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On May&nbsp;30, 2002, Informatica Corporation
(&#147;Informatica&#148;) filed an action against a former
employee of the Company in the United States District Court for
the Northern District of California. The complaint alleged,
among other things, that the employee, previously employed by
Informatica as a district sales manager, misappropriated trade
secrets, principally contact information regarding potential
customers, and
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-39
</FONT>

<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#toc">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>
</DIV>

<P align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED
FINANCIAL&nbsp;STATEMENTS&nbsp;&#151;&nbsp;(Continued)</FONT></B>

<P align="left">
<FONT size="2">unlawfully used that information to benefit the
Company once he joined the Company as a regional sales manager
in January 2002. Informatica sought a temporary restraining
order, which the employee successfully opposed at a hearing held
on June&nbsp;5, 2002. On June&nbsp;24, 2002, the employee
answered the complaint, in which he denied all material
allegations. The employee&#146;s employment by the Company ended
on July&nbsp;5, 2002 after the Company discovered that the
employee had retained certain information from his prior
employment. On July&nbsp;19, 2002, Informatica served an amended
complaint in which it named the Company as a defendant in the
action. The amended complaint alleged misappropriation of trade
secrets, unfair competition, intentional interference with
prospective economic advantage and violation of federal
copyright law, and sought injunctive relief, unspecified damages
and attorneys&#146; fees. On August&nbsp;2, 2002, the Company
served its answer to the amended complaint, in which it denied
these claims. A motion by Informatica for a preliminary
injunction to enjoin the Company from, among other things, using
confidential information brought by the employee to the Company,
was heard on December&nbsp;20, 2002. The court denied
Informatica&#146;s preliminary injunction motion. The Company
has produced information to Informatica which the Company
believes shows there is no merit to Informatica&#146;s claims
against the Company. Among other things, the Company does not
believe that the information retained by the employee
constitutes protectable trade secrets or that any material harm
to Informatica can be demonstrated. In January 2003, Informatica
amended its complaint to add claims against a second Ascential
employee previously employed by Informatica, and against the
Company with respect to actions allegedly taken by such second
employee. The second employee&#146;s employment by the Company
ended on March&nbsp;5, 2003. The Company filed an answer to the
amended complaint, denying all material allegations. The Company
intends to defend itself vigorously.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On August&nbsp;8, 2001, eNet30, Inc.
(&#147;eNet30&#148;) filed suit against the Company in Nevada
state court based on a consulting agreement. eNet30 alleges
claims for breach of contract, breach of the covenant of good
faith and fair dealing, and rescission, and seeks the return of
approximately $0.6&nbsp;million previously paid to the Company.
On September&nbsp;11, 2001, the case was removed to the United
States District Court for the District of Nevada, and on
October&nbsp;22, 2001, the Company filed its answer and
counterclaims against eNet30 for breach of contract, bad faith
and unfair dealing, and unjust enrichment. In May 2002, eNet30
modified its claims to allege that it is entitled to additional
damages based upon allegations of fraud and fraudulent
inducement. The Company believes that eNet30&#146;s claims are
without merit and intends to vigorously defend the suit. On
March&nbsp;28, 2002 eNet30 declared bankruptcy and a trial date
has not yet been set.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">From time to time, in the ordinary course of
business, the Company is involved in various other legal
proceedings and claims, including but not limited to those
related to the operations of the former database business and/or
asserted by former employees of the Company relating to their
employment or compensation by the Company. The Company does not
believe that any of these other proceedings and claims will have
a material adverse effect on the Company&#146;s business or
financial condition.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><FONT size="2">Note 14&nbsp;&#151; Sale of the Database
Business Assets</FONT></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<I><FONT size="2">Holdback. </FONT></I><FONT size="2">On
July&nbsp;1, 2001, the Company completed the initial closing of
the IBM Transaction, which consisted of the sale to IBM of
substantially all of the assets and certain liabilities of the
Company&#146;s database business for $1.0&nbsp;billion in cash.
The IBM Transaction was completed on August&nbsp;1, 2001, upon
the closing of the sale of the assets related to the database
business in the nine remaining countries that were not closed on
July&nbsp;1, 2001. As part of the IBM Transaction, IBM retained
$100.0&nbsp;million of the sale proceeds as a holdback (the
&#147;Holdback&#148;) to satisfy the indemnification obligations
that might arise under the IBM Transaction purchase agreement
(the &#147;MPA&#148;). The MPA provided that the Company would
indemnify IBM and its affiliates against any loss, claim,
damage, liability or other expense incurred in connection with
(i)&nbsp;any failure of any representation or warranty of the
Company to be true and correct in all respects provided,
however, that any such liability, in the aggregate, exceeds on a
cumulative basis $10.0&nbsp;million (and only to the extent of
any such excess); (ii)&nbsp;any breach of any obligation of the
Company; (iii)&nbsp;any of the Excluded Liabilities, as defined
in the MPA; or (iv)&nbsp;the operation or ownership of the
Excluded Assets, as defined in the MPA. The
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-40
</FONT>
<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#toc">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>
</DIV>

<P align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED
FINANCIAL&nbsp;STATEMENTS&nbsp;&#151;&nbsp;(Continued)</FONT></B>

<P align="left">
<FONT size="2">MPA provided that IBM would retain the Holdback
until January&nbsp;1, 2003 except for any funds necessary to
provide for any claims made prior to that date. The MPA also
provided that the Company would receive interest from
July&nbsp;1, 2001 to the payment date of the Holdback. In
January 2003, IBM released the full amount of the Holdback to
the Company, in the amount of $109.3&nbsp;million, which
includes accrued interest. Indemnification obligations of the
Company with respect to certain representations and warranties
under the MPA will terminate on July&nbsp;1, 2003.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The Holdback was recorded as a gain on the sale
of the database business assets during the third quarter of 2001
because at that time the Company believed, beyond a reasonable
doubt, that the entire $100.0&nbsp;million was realizable.
Accordingly, the Company concluded that it was appropriate to
recognize the Holdback amount in the gain and accrue any losses
of the Holdback amount when such losses became probable and
estimable under SFAS&nbsp;5, <I>Accounting for
Contingencies</I>. The Holdback and accrued interest are
included in &#147;Receivable from sale of database
business&#148; on the accompanying consolidated balance sheets
as a component of current assets at December&nbsp;31, 2002 as
the amount was scheduled to be received within one year from the
date of the balance sheet.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">In December 2002, the Company paid to IBM
$4.0&nbsp;million recovered on behalf of IBM from Sprint, one of
the Company&#146;s former customers. The Company also paid IBM,
in December 2002, $3.2&nbsp;million to resolve informal matters
raised by IBM related to the IBM Transaction, which was recorded
as a decrement to &#147;Gain on sale of database business, net
of adjustments&#148;. In conjunction with this transaction, the
Company reconfirmed its continuing obligation to indemnify IBM
for future claims raised pursuant to the MPA and IBM stated that
it was unaware of any such claims at that time. From and after
that time through the date of this filing, IBM has not submitted
any claims pursuant to the indemnification provisions of the MPA.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<I><FONT size="2">Working Capital Adjustment.
</FONT></I><FONT size="2">Under the terms of the MPA, the
Company was obligated to transfer $124.0&nbsp;million in net
working capital to IBM from the database business operations
(the &#147;Working Capital Adjustment&#148;) as of July&nbsp;1,
2001. Working Capital was defined in the MPA as the sum of
(i)&nbsp;net accounts receivable and (ii)&nbsp;prepaid expenses;
minus (a)&nbsp;ordinary course trade payables and
(b)&nbsp;accrued ordinary course expenses (other than any such
expenses incurred in connection with former employees, officer,
directors, or independent contractors). These items were a
subset of all the various assets and liabilities of the database
business that transferred to IBM upon the closing of the IBM
Transaction.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">If the net working capital transferred to IBM on
the closing date exceeded $124.0&nbsp;million, IBM would pay the
Company 50% of the excess over $124.0&nbsp;million. If the net
working capital transferred to IBM was less than
$124.0&nbsp;million, the Company would pay IBM an amount equal
to the shortfall. As of December&nbsp;31, 2001, the Company
expected to be reimbursed at least $3.7&nbsp;million from IBM,
which it estimated to be the minimum net working capital
adjustment. Accordingly, the Company had recognized the minimum
net working capital adjustment of $3.7&nbsp;million in the gain
resulting from the IBM Transaction. On March&nbsp;29, 2002, the
Company was paid $11.0&nbsp;million by IBM as final settlement
of the net working capital adjustment. Accordingly, the Company
recorded the incremental $7.3&nbsp;million received in
&#147;Gain on sale of database business, net of
adjustments&#148; during 2002.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<I><FONT size="2">Gain Adjustment.
</FONT></I><FONT size="2">During 2002, the Company also recorded
a gain adjustment of $0.3&nbsp;million primarily to decrease the
long-term assets sold to IBM and a $1.4&nbsp;million loss to
&#147;Gain on sale of database business, net&#148;. The
$1.4&nbsp;million loss consists of $0.9&nbsp;million of accrued
professional and administrative fees required to dissolve
subsidiaries rendered inactive as a consequence of the IBM
Transaction and $0.5&nbsp;million to adjust the segmentation of
accounts receivable sold to IBM as part of the sale of the
database business assets.
</FONT>

</CENTER>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The database business and the Company&#146;s
ongoing operations were in the same line of business, and the
database business did not represent &#147;a separate major line
of business or class of customer&#148;. Both sold information
management software to medium-sized and large enterprises. In
addition, the product offerings of the two businesses worked
together to form an information management system. The customers
of the Company&#146;s ongoing business needed a database in
order for the Company&#146;s products to perform; therefore, the
target markets for the two businesses were the same. In
addition, the assets, results of operations, and activities of
the database business could not be clearly distinguished from
the other assets, results of operations and activities of the
Company prior to 2001. As such, the sale of the database
business assets to IBM represented the sale of a product line
rather than the disposal of a business segment.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<I><FONT size="2">Accrued IBM Transaction Costs.
</FONT></I><FONT size="2">As a result of the IBM Transaction,
the Company recorded a $41.9&nbsp;million charge during 2001 to
accrue for costs associated with the sale of the database
business assets. The following table sets forth the components
of the accrued transaction costs and related charges incurred
during the twelve months ended December&nbsp;31, 2002 (in
millions):
</FONT>

<CENTER>
<TABLE width="90%" align="center" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="0" border="0">

<TR>

    <TD width="6%"><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
    <TD width="3%"><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>

    <TD width="4%"><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>

<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#toc">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>
</DIV>

<P align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED
FINANCIAL&nbsp;STATEMENTS&nbsp;&#151;&nbsp;(Continued)</FONT></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">Professional fees primarily consist of fees for
investment bankers, attorneys and accountants for services
provided related to the IBM Transaction. Severance and
employment-related charges primarily consist of
(i)&nbsp;severance payments and related taxes for approximately
30 sales and marketing employees and 70 general and
administrative employees of the database business who did not
join IBM after the IBM Transaction, and (ii)&nbsp;a
$4.7&nbsp;million charge that related to the modification of
vesting and exercise terms of stock options for certain
terminated executives and for database employees who joined IBM.
As discussed above, during the third quarter of 2002 the Company
accrued $0.9&nbsp;million of professional and administrative
fees required to dissolve subsidiaries made inactive as a
consequence of the IBM Transaction. During 2002, the Company
reversed $0.9&nbsp;million of accrued severance and related
costs, as certain employees terminated employment voluntarily,
reversed $0.3&nbsp;million of accrued transfer taxes, as the
Company was ultimately required to pay less than what was
originally estimated, and accrued $1.2&nbsp;million of
professional fees to reflect an increase in estimated costs to
reorganize the Company related to the IBM Transaction. As of
December&nbsp;31, 2002, severance payments and related taxes had
been paid to terminate approximately 81 employees. The remaining
accrual balance of $4.4&nbsp;million, which includes severance
and related taxes, is expected be paid on various dates
extending through 2006. The remaining accrual balance of
$1.2&nbsp;million for professional fees is expected be paid by
June&nbsp;30, 2003.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<I><FONT size="2">Transition Services and Other.
</FONT></I><FONT size="2">The Company and IBM are providing
certain transition services to each other for a limited period
of time following the closing of the IBM Transaction. These
services mainly consist of the performance of certain
administrative functions, hosting systems and the provision of
office space in shared facilities of which one of the parties is
the primary leaseholder. To facilitate the provision of these
services, at the time of the IBM Transaction, the Company and
IBM entered into reciprocal transitional service agreements,
under which both parties provide and utilize transitional
services at agreed upon rates that the Company believes
represent the fair value of such services. During the year ended
December&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company provided to IBM and
purchased from IBM transitional services amounting to
$3.8&nbsp;million and $2.5&nbsp;million, respectively. During
the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2001, the Company provided
transitional services to IBM totaling $2.6&nbsp;million and
purchased transitional services from IBM totaling
$4.1&nbsp;million. The provision for shared administrative
functions and office space in shared facilities ended during the
second quarter of 2002. Effective December&nbsp;31, 2002, the
provision of shared infrastructure cost was substantially
completed, except with respect to certain software support
functions that have been extended through March&nbsp;31, 2003.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended March&nbsp;31,
2002, the Company paid IBM $13.2 million to fund the transfer of
certain employee related accruals, $2.9&nbsp;million to buy out
certain lease obligations, $2.0&nbsp;million to pay for the
transfer of certain long-term assets and $2.4&nbsp;million for
royalties and other obligations owed to IBM. In conjunction with
the payments made to IBM, IBM paid the Company
$21.6&nbsp;million to fund certain severance costs,
$11.0&nbsp;million to settle the net working capital adjustment
under the Agreement and $0.8&nbsp;million for taxes and other
fees.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><FONT size="2">Note 15&nbsp;&#151; Termination of the Content
Management Product Line</FONT></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On January&nbsp;22, 2002, the Company&#146;s
board of directors endorsed the decision to divest the
Company&#146;s content management product line because it did
not align with the Company&#146;s strategic goals. The net
assets of the product line were available for immediate sale and
an investment bank was engaged to assist in the divestiture
process. At the time, the Company intended to complete the
divestiture as soon as possible and, in any event, within one
year of the Company&#146;s decision to sell the product line.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">As of March&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company believed
that the actions taken by the Company met the criteria under
SFAS No.&nbsp;144 for classification of a disposal group as
&#147;Assets held for sale&#148; and &#147;Liabilities
associated with assets held for sale&#148; and used this
presentation on the balance sheet in the report filed on
Form&nbsp;10-Q for the three months ended March&nbsp;31, 2002.
Accordingly, the Company ceased amortizing capitalized software
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-43
</FONT>

<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#toc">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>

<P align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED
FINANCIAL&nbsp;STATEMENTS&nbsp;&#151;&nbsp;(Continued)</FONT></B>

<P align="left">
<FONT size="2">costs as of January&nbsp;22, 2002. The Company
has included the operating results of this product line for the
year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002, as a component of
&#147;Operating loss&#148; in the Condensed Consolidated
Statement of Operations. The Company was unable to distinguish
certain historical financial information associated with this
product line prior to January 2002; accordingly, the Company has
not presented historical results as discontinued operations in
accordance with the provisions of SFAS No.&nbsp;144. The assets
related to the content management product line were evaluated by
the Company and were carried at cost, which was less than the
estimated fair market value net of costs to sell. As of
May&nbsp;15, 2002, the Company did not expect to record a loss
on the divestiture of its content management product line.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During June 2002, the Company ceased efforts to
find a buyer for its content management product line as no
interested buyers had been identified. During the three months
ended June&nbsp;30, 2002, in conjunction with its decision to
cease efforts to find a buyer, the Company terminated associated
operations, except for completion of previously committed
consulting and support contracts, and recorded charges totaling
$7.3&nbsp;million consisting of $4.5&nbsp;million categorized as
&#147;Cost of software&#148; for the impairment of software
costs previously capitalized and charges totaling
$2.8&nbsp;million as &#147;Merger, realignment, and other
charges&#148;. The $2.8&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger,
realignment, and other charges&#148; includes $1.4&nbsp;million
of severance costs, $0.8&nbsp;million of equipment impairment
costs and $0.6&nbsp;million of other exit costs. During the
three months ended September&nbsp;30, 2002, the Company recorded
additional charges totaling $0.5&nbsp;million as &#147;Merger,
realignment, and other charges&#148; comprised of
$0.3&nbsp;million of severance costs and $0.2&nbsp;million of
other exit costs. The components of these charges are discussed
in detail below (see Note&nbsp;16 in these Notes to Consolidated
Financial Statements). In addition, the Company recorded
$1.7&nbsp;million in bad debt expense for the year ended
December&nbsp;31, 2002 related to the write off of accounts
receivable balances that were deemed uncollectible as a result
of the termination of this product line.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The following section summarizes, by quarter, the
net merger, realignment and other expense of $23.7&nbsp;million
for the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002, of which
$20.1&nbsp;million will be paid in cash.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended December&nbsp;31,
2002, the Company recorded $4.7&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger,
realignment and other&#148; charges, $2.3&nbsp;million related
to its third quarter 2002 realignment plan, $2.0&nbsp;million
related to revised assumptions for facility and equipment
charges resulting from the IBM Transaction, $0.2&nbsp;million
related to revised assumptions for facility charges resulting
from the 2000 strategic realignment and $0.2&nbsp;million in
other facility exit costs. The Company also reversed
$0.4&nbsp;million of other exit costs related primarily to the
$50.0&nbsp;million merger reserve recorded in 2000 in
conjunction with the acquisition of Ardent that is no longer
required. The net impact of these activities was a
$4.3&nbsp;million charge recorded to the &#147;Merger,
realignment and other&#148; line of the Company&#146;s
consolidated statements of operations.
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-44
</FONT>
<!-- PAGEBREAK -->
<P><HR noshade><P>
<H5 align="left" style="page-break-before:always"><A HREF="#toc">Table of Contents</A></H5><P>

<DIV align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">ASCENTIAL SOFTWARE CORPORATION</FONT></B>
</DIV>

<P align="center">
<B><FONT size="2">NOTES TO CONSOLIDATED
FINANCIAL&nbsp;STATEMENTS&nbsp;&#151;&nbsp;(Continued)</FONT></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended September&nbsp;30,
2002, the Company recorded $8.2&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger,
realignment and other&#148; charges, $7.8&nbsp;million related
to its third quarter 2002 realignment plan and $0.4&nbsp;million
related to revised assumptions for facility and equipment
charges resulting from the IBM Transaction. The Company also
reversed $0.5&nbsp;million related to the facility and equipment
charges resulting from the IBM Transaction for a reserve on a
facility lease deposit that was refunded in full. The net impact
of these activities was a $7.7&nbsp;million charge recorded to
the &#147;Merger, realignment and other&#148; line of the
Company&#146;s consolidated statements of operations.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended June&nbsp;30, 2002,
the Company recorded $7.7&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger,
realignment and other&#148; charges related to its second
quarter 2002 realignment plan and reversed $0.4&nbsp;million
related to accrued severance payments that were originally
recorded as part of its third quarter of 2001 realignment plan
as certain employees voluntarily terminated employment, and
reversed $0.3 of an accrual related to the Facility and
Equipment charges resulting from the IBM Transaction due to
revised assumptions utilized in estimating facility exit costs.
The net impact of these activities was a $7.0&nbsp;million
charge recorded to the &#147;Merger, realignment and other&#148;
line of the Company&#146;s consolidated statements of operations.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended March&nbsp;31,
2002, the Company recorded $4.7&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger,
realignment and other&#148; charges. This $4.7&nbsp;million
charge consisted of $2.2&nbsp;million of facility and equipment
charges resulting from the IBM Transaction, as described below.
The remaining balance of $2.5&nbsp;million consisted of
$1.6&nbsp;million of severance and associated benefits,
$0.8&nbsp;million of facilities costs and $0.1&nbsp;million of
other exit costs that were incurred and paid during the first
three months of 2002.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The components of the Company&#146;s net merger,
realignment and other charges are outlined below.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Third Quarter
2002 Realignment</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended September&nbsp;30,
2002, the Company approved a plan to further realign its
infrastructure by reducing its workforce. The Company also
identified certain assets associated with vacated facilities and
headcount reductions that no longer had value at
September&nbsp;30, 2002. This plan relates to a continuing
effort to reduce the Company&#146;s infrastructure costs, the
IBM Transaction (see Note 14 in these Notes to Consolidated
Financial Statements), and the termination of its content
management product line (see Note 15 in these Notes to
Consolidated Financial Statements). As a result, the Company
recorded $10.1&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger, realignment and
other&#148; charges during the six months ended
December&nbsp;31, 2002. This $10.1&nbsp;million charge consisted
of $6.6&nbsp;million to reduce the Company&#146;s infrastructure
costs, $3.0&nbsp;million related to the IBM Transaction and
$0.5&nbsp;million related to the termination of its content
management product line. The $6.6&nbsp;million charge related to
realigning the Company&#146;s infrastructure to reduce costs
consists of $6.5&nbsp;million to terminate 142 employees and
$0.1&nbsp;million of other exit costs. The $3.0&nbsp;million
charge related to exiting facilities in connection with the IBM
Transaction consists of $2.7&nbsp;million to write-off abandoned
fixed assets, primarily at vacated facilities and $0.3 of
additional facility lease costs. The $0.5&nbsp;million charge
related to the termination of the content management product
line consists of $0.3&nbsp;million to terminate
17&nbsp;employees and $0.2&nbsp;million of other exit costs. The
159 employees being terminated as part of the third quarter 2002
realignment includes approximately 98 sales and marketing
employees, 17&nbsp;general and administrative employees,
12&nbsp;research and development employees, and
32&nbsp;professional services employees. The following
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-45

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The unpaid severance balance of $1.3&nbsp;million
as of December&nbsp;31, 2002 is expected to be paid by
September&nbsp;30, 2003 and relates primarily to bi-monthly
severance payments for employees who have already been
terminated and severance payments for employees who, barring
unforeseen circumstances, are expected to be terminated by the
end of September&nbsp;30, 2003.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Second
Quarter 2002 Realignment</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended June&nbsp;30, 2002,
the Company approved plans to terminate its content management
product line (see Note&nbsp;15 in these Notes to Consolidated
Financial Statements), and realign its infrastructure by
reducing its workforce and closing facilities in order to reduce
costs. In addition, the Company terminated 91 employees
subsequent to the April&nbsp;3, 2002 acquisition of Vality. As a
result, the Company recorded $7.7&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger,
realignment and other&#148; charges during the period. This
$7.7&nbsp;million charge consisted of $4.1&nbsp;million of
charges during the period related to the integration of Vality,
$2.8&nbsp;million to terminate operations related to the
Company&#146;s content management product line, and $0.8 of
additional costs associated with the wind down of the database
business. The $4.1&nbsp;million charge to integrate Vality
consisted of $4.0&nbsp;million to terminate 91 Ascential
employees and $0.1&nbsp;million of other costs. The
$2.8&nbsp;million charge related to its content management
product line consisted of $1.4&nbsp;million in severance costs
to terminate 52&nbsp;employees, $0.8&nbsp;million in computer
equipment impairments, $0.4&nbsp;million of professional fees
incurred in the attempt to sell the product line and $0.2
million of other exit costs. The 143 employees terminated
included 60 sales and marketing employees, nine general and
administrative employees, 27 research and development employees,
and 47 professional services employees. The $0.8&nbsp;million of
costs associated with the database business were comprised of
$0.4&nbsp;million of facilities costs, $0.3&nbsp;million of
severance costs, and $0.1&nbsp;million of other costs. The
following table sets forth the significant components of the
charge recognized during the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002
and the accrued balance remaining at December&nbsp;31, 2002 (in
millions):
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;First Quarter
2002 Realignment</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the three months ended March&nbsp;31,
2002, the Company approved plans to continue to reduce costs
from its Informix Software business due to the IBM Transaction
and consolidate certain offices in Europe. As a result the
Company recorded $2.5&nbsp;million of &#147;Merger, realignment
and other&#148; charges consisting of $1.6&nbsp;million of
severance and employment related costs to terminate 35 sales and
marketing employees, $0.8&nbsp;million for lease management and
other lease obligation costs and $0.1&nbsp;million of other exit
costs.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Third Quarter
2001 Realignment</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the quarter ended September&nbsp;30, 2001,
the Company approved plans to reduce its worldwide headcount and
as a result recorded a $12.0&nbsp;million charge to
&#147;Merger, realignment and other&#148; in 2001. The worldwide
workforce reduction started in the third quarter of 2001 and has
included a reduction of approximately 180 sales and marketing
employees, 20 general and administrative employees, 10 research
and development employees and 50 professional services and
manufacturing employees. As a result, the Company recorded
realignment and other charges of $12.0&nbsp;million. The
following table sets forth the accrual activity for the year
ended December&nbsp;31, 2002 (in millions):
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">Severance and employment-related costs primarily
consisted of termination compensation and related benefits for
employees. During the quarter ended June&nbsp;30, 2002, the
Company reversed $0.4&nbsp;million of the accrual related to
approximately 18 employees, as it was no longer required. As of
December&nbsp;31, 2002, termination compensation and related
benefits had been paid to terminate 242 employees.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Facility and
Equipment Charges resulting from IBM Transaction</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">As a result of the IBM Transaction (see Note 14
in these Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements) the Company
no longer required as much facility space and recorded a
$35.3&nbsp;million charge to &#147;Merger, realignment and
other&#148; during 2001 for facilities and equipment costs
related to the Company&#146;s vacant, or partially vacant,
facilities at December&nbsp;31, 2001. This charge was comprised
of a reserve for residual lease obligations and restoration
costs and write-offs related to leasehold improvements and other
fixed assets at
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-47
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The $13.0&nbsp;million of residual lease
obligations is comprised of $11.5 million of lease-related
payments that the Company estimates it will make from the date
of the Company&#146;s exit from each facility to the end of the
corresponding lease term, net of rental payments from IBM or
other sub&#150;lessees, and $1.5&nbsp;million of estimated
restoration costs for facilities that the Company has either
exited or finalized plans to exit. The unpaid residual lease
obligations and restoration costs of $13.0&nbsp;million at
December&nbsp;31, 2002 relates to facilities that the Company
has exited or plans to exit at December&nbsp;31, 2002 and expire
from 2002 through 2008.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">On March&nbsp;31, 2002, the Company paid IBM
$2.9&nbsp;million for a release from lease obligations in seven
facilities located in the United States and the United Kingdom
while assuming additional lease obligations for two facilities
in Ireland and Germany. The result of the negotiated release was
a reduction in the Company&#146;s accrual for previously
estimated lease obligations for the seven facilities by an
aggregate of $7.0&nbsp;million while increasing its previously
estimated obligation by $2.0&nbsp;million for the facilities in
Ireland and Germany, resulting in a $5.0&nbsp;million net
reduction in the total charge.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002, the
Company revised the assumptions used to calculate the estimate
of residual lease obligations and restoration costs for its
other remaining properties that resulted in $9.3 million of
adjustments to the Company&#146;s facilities accrual. The
majority of the adjustment relates to decreasing market prices
for sublease rental receipts, difficulty in obtaining sublease
tenants, primarily in the United States and Europe, and
additional costs arising from negotiations to exit facilities
prior to the end of the lease term. In total, barring unforeseen
circumstances, the Company estimated it will receive
$11.6&nbsp;million of sublease income, $2.8&nbsp;million for
properties already sublet and $8.8&nbsp;million from properties
where a sublet is anticipated. The Company&#146;s sublease
estimates assume that no subtenants will default on rental
payments and that the Company will be able to obtain subtenants
at local market rates where market conditions are considered
favorable. Sublease assumptions also include a vacancy period
from the time the Company vacates the facility to the time the
subtenant begins to pay rent. The following sets forth the
components of the charges recorded to &#147;Merger, realignment
and other charges&#148; during the year ended December&nbsp;31,
2002 (in millions):
</FONT>

adjustments or charges in the future due to changes in estimates
arising from the size and quantity of its facilities that are
being exited and the volatility of the real estate markets in
which the Company&#146;s facilities are located. As of
December&nbsp;31, 2002, barring unforeseen circumstances, the
Company does not expect future charges related to undiscounted
lease obligations,

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;2000
Strategic Realignment</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the quarter ended September&nbsp;30, 2000,
the Company approved plans to realign its operations by
establishing two operating businesses and as a result recorded a
charge of $86.9&nbsp;million to &#147;Merger, realignment and
other&#148; in 2000. The strategic realignment included a
refinement of the Company&#146;s product strategy, consolidation
of facilities and operations to improve efficiency and a
reduction in worldwide headcount of approximately 310 sales and
marketing employees, 120 general and administrative employees,
260 research and development employees and 100 professional
services and manufacturing employees. During 2002 the Company
recorded $0.2&nbsp;million of additional costs to exit a
facility in the United States arising from negotiations to
terminate the lease prior to the end of the lease term.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;

the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2002 (in millions):

    <TD><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
    <TD><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
    <TD><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
    <TD align="left"><HR size="4" noshade></TD>

    <TD><FONT size="2">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>

</TR>

</TABLE>
</CENTER>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">The remaining accrual balance at
December&nbsp;31, 2002 consists of $0.6 million for lease
obligations and $0.1&nbsp;million of restoration costs for a
redundant facility and will be paid by June&nbsp;30, 2003.
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<B><I><FONT size="2">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;Ardent Merger
and Other</FONT></I></B>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">As a result of the merger with Ardent Software,
Inc. in March 2000 and various merger and realignment activities
that occurred prior to 2000, the Company had recorded charges of
$59.7&nbsp;million to &#147;Merger, realignment and other&#148;
arising from decisions to exit certain facilities. During 2002
the Company reversed $0.4&nbsp;million of other exit costs
associated with the Ardent acquisition as they were no longer
needed. As of December&nbsp;31, 2002, approximately
$0.5&nbsp;million of facility exit costs remained unpaid. Of the
$0.5&nbsp;million in facility exist costs, $0.3&nbsp;million are
for residual lease obligations and $0.2&nbsp;million for
restoration costs on idle facilities that expire at various
dates through 2004.
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-49
</FONT>

<P align="left">
<HR size="1" width="18%" align="left" noshade>
<P>

<TABLE width="100%" border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0">

<TR>
    <TD width="4%"></TD>
    <TD width="96%"></TD>
</TR>

<TR valign="top">
    <TD><FONT size="2">(1)&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
    <TD align="left">
    <FONT size="2">Amounts may vary from annual totals due to
    rounding.
    </FONT></TD>
</TR>

</TABLE>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">During the closing for the quarter ended
December&nbsp;31, 2001, the Company discovered errors in
previously reported net income for the three-month period ended
September&nbsp;30, 2001. The errors relate to:
(1)&nbsp;$1.2&nbsp;million of tangible assets that IBM purchased
that were included on the Company&#146;s balance sheet at
September&nbsp;30, 2001 and that were identified and transferred
to IBM during the fourth quarter of 2001; and
(2)&nbsp;$4.5&nbsp;million of additional obligations relating to
facilities that the Company has either permanently exited or has
plans to permanently exit resulting from the IBM Transaction. As
a result, previously reported net income, and basic and diluted
earnings per share of $651.4 million, $2.34 and $2.28,
respectively, for the quarter ended September&nbsp;30, 2001 have
been restated to $645.7&nbsp;million, $2.32 and $2.26,
respectively.
</FONT>

<P align="left">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;
<FONT size="2">In addition, in the 2001&nbsp;year-end financial
statements, the Company reclassified $21.4&nbsp;million of
facilities and equipment costs related to the IBM Transaction
from the Gain on sale of database business line item to the
Merger, realignment and other charges line item. (See
Note&nbsp;14 to Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements).
</FONT>

<P align="center"><FONT size="2">F-51
